title: Conversion and Hierarchy
description: Understand the rules for converting leads and the account hierarchy logic for different customer scenarios.

sections.title: Conversion and Hierarchy
sections.content:
When a lead is qualified and ready to move forward, it converts into the account hierarchy. This is a critical step because the conversion logic determines the structure of the account, the parent/child relationships, and ultimately which account will own the opportunity. Conversion is not a simple one-to-one transaction—it follows specific rules based on the nature of the prospect and the existing customer base.

**The Four Conversion Scenarios**:

**Scenario 1: Net-New Prospect** 
The prospect is a new company that NxN has never done business with. The prospect's company name does not match any existing account in Salesforce. In this case, conversion creates a new Parent Account record (representing the entire enterprise) and a new Entity Child Account (representing the specific business unit or delivery entity that will consume the service). The contact from the lead converts to a Contact record linked to the Parent Account. The prospect is now set up to have an opportunity created on the Entity Child Account.

**Scenario 2: Existing Account with No NxN Entity Child**
The prospect works for a company that already exists in Salesforce as a Parent Account (perhaps because NxN already has a relationship with another division), but there is no Entity Child Account for the specific business unit that this prospect represents. In this case, conversion links the lead to the existing Parent Account, creates a new Entity Child Account for the prospect's business unit, and converts the contact to a Contact record. This ensures that both the existing relationship and the new business unit are properly represented in the hierarchy.

**Scenario 3: Existing Account with Existing NxN Entity Child**
The prospect works for a company that already exists in Salesforce and already has an Entity Child Account for the prospect's business unit. The prospect may be a new contact within that same business unit, or may be a contact we've already engaged with. In this case, conversion links the lead to the existing Parent Account and existing Entity Child Account, and either creates a new Contact record or links to an existing Contact record. No new accounts are created; the lead simply becomes part of the existing account hierarchy.

**Scenario 4: Partner-Led or Managed Account**
Some accounts are partner-led, meaning that a partner organization has the primary commercial relationship and NxN is the underlying service provider. In this case, conversion may not create a standard Parent/Entity Child hierarchy. Instead, the lead converts to a Partner or Reseller relationship, where the partner account is the Parent and NxN's role is tracked through a Partner status or flag. Alternatively, a parent partner account may be created, with NxN's own Entity Child Account beneath it.

**Account Hierarchy Structure**:

The account hierarchy in Salesforce is designed to support complex organizations:

- **Parent Account**: Represents the entire enterprise or holding company. This is the highest level in the hierarchy. If NxN works with multiple divisions of the same parent company, they all share the same Parent Account.
- **Entity Child Account**: Represents the specific business unit, subsidiary, or delivery entity that consumes NxN's services. This is the account on which opportunities are created. Multiple Entity Child Accounts can exist under a single Parent Account if NxN serves multiple divisions or subsidiaries.
- **Other Child Accounts**: Some organizations may have additional levels (e.g., departments, regional offices, or specific project entities) represented as additional child accounts below the Entity Child Account.

**Contact Management During Conversion**:

During conversion, the contact from the lead is converted to a Contact record. This contact is typically linked to the Parent Account, but if multiple Entity Child Accounts exist, it's important to track which contacts are relevant to which business units. A contact may be linked to a Parent Account and associated with one or more Entity Child Accounts through role or relationship fields.

**Rules for Determining the Correct Hierarchy**:

When converting a lead, the system follows these rules to determine the correct hierarchy:

1. **Company Name Matching**: The lead's company name is matched against existing Parent Accounts using fuzzy matching logic. Similar names are flagged for manual review. Exact matches are automatically linked.
2. **Company Domain / Website**: If the lead's email domain or company website matches an existing account, that account is considered a match.
3. **Manual Override**: If automated matching is uncertain, a human reviewer (from the CRM/Governance team or Sales team) can manually select the correct Parent Account or indicate that a new account should be created.
4. **Entity Child Selection**: Once the Parent Account is confirmed, the system checks whether an Entity Child Account exists for the prospect's stated business unit or division. If one exists, the lead converts into that Entity Child. If not, a new Entity Child is created.

**Why This Matters**:

Correct hierarchy conversion is critical because it ensures that:
- Opportunities are created on the right account, so revenue is attributed correctly.
- Account managers can see all activity related to a parent company, even if multiple divisions are involved.
- Duplicate accounts are prevented, so the database remains clean.
- Reporting accurately reflects the organization's customer base and pipeline.

Incorrect hierarchy conversions lead to duplicates, misattributed revenue, confused reporting, and account manager frustration. Take the time to verify the correct Parent Account and Entity Child Account during lead conversion, and your entire system will be more accurate.
